mnemonics: Early Bird vs. Late Sleeper: Early binding (static) is decided at compile-time like an early bird with a fixed plan, while late binding (dynamic) is decided at run-time like a late sleeper who decides what to do when they wake up.
V-TABLE for Victory: Remember that Virtual functions (late binding) use a V-table (Virtual Table) to achieve their dynamic behavior.
keyPoints: Early binding (static binding) occurs at compile-time. The function call is resolved to a specific function definition based on the static type of the object (the type used in the declaration).

Late binding (dynamic binding) occurs at run-time. The function call is resolved using the V-table, based on the dynamic type of the object (the actual type of the object being pointed to or referenced).
The 'virtual' keyword in a base class is what enables late binding for a specific function. Without it, the function will use early binding, even if the object is a derived type.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to use the 'virtual' keyword in the base class function, which means the function will not be bound polymorphically and the base class version will always be called.

Assuming that a destructor does not need to be virtual when a base class pointer is used to delete a derived object, which leads to undefined behavior and potential memory leaks because the derived class destructor won't be called.
